---
title: "Kaplan-Meier Survivorship Analysis"
author: "Steven Roberts"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: false
    embed-resources: true
editor: visual
---

BACKGROUND - https://sr320.github.io/tumbling-oysters/posts/73-sormi-mort/



```{=html}
<!--
================================================================================
REUSABLE KAPLAN-MEIER SURVIVORSHIP TEMPLATE
================================================================================
Adapted from A. Huffmyer's Manchester stress-survival analysis:
https://ahuffmyer.github.io/posts/2026-06-03-analyzing-manchester-stress-survival.html

This template builds Kaplan-Meier survival curves and (optionally) a Cox
Proportional Hazards model from binary mortality data recorded over time.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
HOW TO FORMAT YOUR INPUT DATA  (read this before running)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The template expects a WIDE csv: one row per individual, one column per
time point, plus one or more grouping (treatment/factor) columns.

REQUIRED LAYOUT
  - Each ROW = one individual (one oyster, fish, plant, animal, etc.).
  - One or more GROUPING columns (e.g. Treatment, Family, Tank). These are the
    factors you want to compare survival across.
  - One column PER TIME POINT. The COLUMN NAME must be the numeric time value
    (e.g. 0, 6, 12, 24, 54 -> hours; or 0, 1, 2, 3 -> days).
  - Each time-point CELL holds the status at that time:
        0 = alive
        1 = dead
    Once an individual dies it should stay 1 (dead) for all later time points,
    OR be left blank after death (see NOTE on censoring below).

EXAMPLE (hours 0 - 54; two grouping columns):

  ID , Hardening_Treatment , Family , 0 , 6 , 12 , 24 , 36 , 48 , 54
  A1 , Control             , 087    , 0 , 0 , 0  , 1  , 1  , 1  , 1
  A2 , Control             , 087    , 0 , 0 , 0  , 0  , 0  , 0  , 0
  A3 , Hardened            , 153    , 0 , 0 , 1  , 1  , 1  , 1  , 1
  ...

KEY RULES
  - Column headers for time points must be plain numbers (0, 6, 12 ...). Do not
    write "0h" or "T0" -- the code converts these headers to numeric time.
  - Grouping column names must be valid (no spaces is safest, e.g.
    "Hardening_Treatment" not "Hardening Treatment").
  - Use consistent, evenly-interpretable time units (all hours OR all days).
  - The LAST time point is the end of observation. An individual still 0 (alive)
    at the last time point is treated as surviving (right-censored) at that time.

NOTE on event/censoring coding:
  The Surv(time, status) call below uses status as the EVENT indicator where
  1 = event (death) and 0 = censored/alive. The pivot+slice logic below converts
  your wide 0/1 grid into the single (time, status) row per individual that the
  survival package needs: time = first time the individual was recorded dead
  (status=1), or the final time point if it never died (status=0).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
WHAT TO EDIT  (search for "# >>> EDIT")
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  1. data_path        - path to your csv
  2. group_vars       - your grouping/factor column(s)
  3. id_var           - your individual ID column (optional but recommended)
  4. first_time / last_time column names handled automatically via group_vars
  5. axis labels / title / colors in the plotting chunks
================================================================================
-->
```

## Set up

```{r setup, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Install any missing packages (uncomment to run once):
# install.packages(c("tidyverse","survival","readxl","ggsurvfit",
#                    "gtsummary","cardx","broom.helpers","cowplot"))
# Note: tbl_regression() needs broom.helpers (>= 1.20.0) and cardx.
 
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(survival)
library(readxl)
library(ggsurvfit)
library(gtsummary)
library(cardx)
library(cowplot)
```

```{r config}
# >>> EDIT: analysis configuration ---------------------------------------------

# Path to your wide-format mortality csv (see header for required layout)
data_path  <- "../data/20260604-36C-USDA-families/survivorship.csv"

# Grouping / factor column(s) to compare survival across.
# Use one or more; e.g. c("Hardening_Treatment", "Family")
group_vars <- c("Family")

# Individual ID column. Set to NULL if you do not have one.
id_var     <- "ID"

# ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
```

## Read in data

```{r read-data, message=FALSE}
data_wide <- readr::read_csv(data_path)

# Quick look at the raw wide data
head(data_wide)
glimpse(data_wide)
```

## Reshape to one (time, status) row per individual

```{r reshape}
# Identify the time-point columns: every column whose name is a number.
time_cols <- names(data_wide)[!is.na(suppressWarnings(as.numeric(names(data_wide))))]

stopifnot("No numeric time-point columns found -- check that your time columns are named 0,6,12,... " = length(time_cols) > 0)

# Long format: one row per individual x time point
data_long <- data_wide %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols      = all_of(time_cols),
    names_to  = "time",
    values_to = "status"
  ) %>%
  mutate(time = as.numeric(time)) %>%
  arrange(across(all_of(c(id_var, group_vars))), time)

# Collapse to ONE row per individual:
#   - if the individual ever died (status == 1), keep the FIRST death time
#   - otherwise keep the LAST observed time point with status = 0 (censored)
grouping_keys <- c(id_var, group_vars)
grouping_keys <- grouping_keys[!sapply(grouping_keys, is.null)]

data <- data_long %>%
  group_by(across(all_of(grouping_keys))) %>%
  summarise(
    time   = if (any(status == 1, na.rm = TRUE)) min(time[status == 1], na.rm = TRUE)
             else max(time, na.rm = TRUE),
    status = as.integer(any(status == 1, na.rm = TRUE)),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Make grouping columns factors for clean legends
data <- data %>% mutate(across(all_of(group_vars), as.factor))

head(data)
```

```{=html}
<!--
If your data already has ONE row per individual with explicit `time` and
`status` columns, you can skip the reshape above and simply do:
    data <- read_csv(data_path)
-->
```

## Generate Kaplan-Meier survival curves

```{r km-fit}
# Build the survival formula from your grouping variables, e.g.
#   Surv(time, status) ~ Hardening_Treatment + Family
km_formula <- as.formula(
  paste("Surv(time, status) ~", paste(group_vars, collapse = " + "))
)

s1 <- survival::survfit(km_formula, data = data)
summary(s1)
```

```{r km-plot-combined, warning=FALSE}
ggsurvfit::survfit2(km_formula, data = data) %>%
  ggsurvfit::ggsurvfit() +
  labs(
    x = "Time",                # >>> EDIT: e.g. "Hours" or "Days"
    y = "Survival probability"
  ) +
  ylim(0, 1)
```

## Cox Proportional Hazards model (optional)

```{r cox-model}
# Interaction model across grouping variables, e.g.
#   Surv(time, status) ~ Hardening_Treatment * Family
cox_formula <- as.formula(
  paste("Surv(time, status) ~", paste(group_vars, collapse = " * "))
)

cox_fit <- survival::coxph(cox_formula, data = data)
cox_fit

# Tidy table with hazard ratios (exp = TRUE)
cox_fit %>% tbl_regression(exp = TRUE)
```

```{=html}
<!--
Interpretation guide:
  - Hazard ratio (HR) > 1  -> higher risk of death (lower survival)
  - Hazard ratio (HR) < 1  -> lower risk of death (higher survival)
  - A significant interaction term means group effects depend on each other
    (e.g. treatment effect differs by family).
-->
```

## Publication plots
 
```{r theme}
my_theme <- theme_classic() +
  theme(
    axis.title.y = element_text(size = 14, color = "black"),
    axis.text.y  = element_text(size = 12, color = "black"),
    axis.text.x  = element_text(size = 12, color = "black"),
    axis.title.x = element_text(size = 14, color = "black"),
    legend.position = "none"
  )
```

```{r plot-by-group1, warning=FALSE}
# Survival by the FIRST grouping variable
g1 <- group_vars[1]
f1 <- as.formula(paste("Surv(time, status) ~", g1))
 
# Build a color palette sized to the number of groups so it never runs short.
# >>> EDIT: replace `group_colors` with your own vector to set exact colors,
#     e.g. group_colors <- c("darkgray", "orange")  (must have >= n_groups values)
n_groups1     <- nlevels(factor(data[[g1]]))
group_colors1 <- scales::hue_pal()(n_groups1)   # auto palette; swap for your own
 
plot1 <- ggsurvfit::survfit2(f1, data = data) %>%
  ggsurvfit::ggsurvfit() +
  labs(x = "Time", y = "Survival probability", title = "") +  # >>> EDIT axis label
  ylim(0, 1) +
  my_theme +
  scale_colour_manual(values = group_colors1) +
  theme(legend.position = "right")
 
plot1
```
 
```{r plot-by-group2, warning=FALSE}
# Survival by the SECOND grouping variable.
# The if-guard means this chunk is safe to run even with a single grouping var.
plot2 <- NULL
if (length(group_vars) >= 2) {
  g2 <- group_vars[2]
  f2 <- as.formula(paste("Surv(time, status) ~", g2))
 
  n_groups2     <- nlevels(factor(data[[g2]]))
  group_colors2 <- scales::hue_pal()(n_groups2)
 
  plot2 <- ggsurvfit::survfit2(f2, data = data) %>%
    ggsurvfit::ggsurvfit() +
    labs(x = "Time", y = "Survival probability", title = "") +  # >>> EDIT axis label
    ylim(0, 1) +
    my_theme +
    scale_colour_manual(values = group_colors2) +
    theme(legend.position = "right")
 
  print(plot2)
} else {
  message("Only one grouping variable supplied -- skipping second-group plot.")
}
```
 
```{r combine-plots, warning=FALSE}
# Side-by-side panel (only when a second plot exists)
if (!is.null(plot2)) {
  cowplot::plot_grid(plot1, plot2, labels = c("A", "B"), ncol = 2)
}
```
 
```{r save-plots, eval=FALSE}
# >>> EDIT: uncomment / adjust to save figures
 #ggsave("../outputs/01-mgig-heat-survivorship-20260604/survival_by_treatment.png", plot1, width = 6, height = 4, dpi = 300)
#ggsave("../outputs/01-mgig-heat-survivorship-20260604/survival_by_family.png",    plot2, width = 6, height = 4, dpi = 300)
```
 
## Take homes
 
<!-- >>> EDIT: write your interpretation here. Examples:
- Was there an effect of treatment on survival?
- Did groups (e.g. families) differ in susceptibility?
- Any interaction effects?
-->
